content.text: AI repository secrets — LLM provider API keys, vector database credentials, embedding service tokens, and evaluation platform API keys — are high-value targets because a single compromised LLM API key can be used to generate millions of dollars of API spend within hours, exfiltrate training data, or bypass safety controls by calling the API directly without the application's guardrails. Secrets scanning CI gates prevent these credentials from entering the repository's git history in the first place, and detect and block them at every subsequent layer if they do.
What it is

Secrets scanning is the automated detection of credentials, API keys, tokens, and other sensitive values in source code, configuration files, and git history. A secrets scanning CI gate runs this detection as a mandatory step in the CI pipeline — on pull request creation, on every commit push, and optionally as a pre-commit hook on the developer's local machine — and blocks merges or builds when secrets are found.

For AI repositories, the set of secrets to detect is larger than for conventional codebases. In addition to standard secrets (AWS credentials, GitHub tokens, database passwords), AI repositories contain: LLM provider API keys (OpenAI sk-..., Anthropic sk-ant-..., Google AI, Mistral, Cohere, Together AI), vector store access tokens (Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant), embedding service keys (OpenAI embeddings, Voyage AI), evaluation platform keys (LangSmith, PromptLayer), Pact Broker tokens, and HuggingFace access tokens with model download permissions.

Secrets scanning operates in two modes: pattern matching (regular expressions that match known secret formats for specific providers) and entropy analysis (detecting high-entropy strings that may be random tokens or keys even if they do not match a known pattern). Modern scanners like truffleHog and gitleaks use both modes in combination and maintain curated detector libraries updated when new API providers launch.
Why it matters

API key exposure in public repositories is the most common and most immediately impactful security incident for AI teams. A leaked OpenAI API key posted to a public GitHub repository is typically detected by automated credential-harvesting bots within seconds of the push. The key is then used for cryptocurrency mining prompts, model API abuse, and in some cases attempts to exfiltrate training data or fine-tuning datasets if the key has organization-level permissions.

Private repositories are not safe either. Internal repositories may be compromised through phishing, supply chain attacks, or inadvertent public exposure. A secrets scanning gate ensures that even if the repository is accessed by an unauthorized party, the credentials stored in history are already revoked (because the gate caught them at commit time) rather than remaining valid and exploitable.

The economic impact of a leaked LLM API key is disproportionately large compared to other credential types. An AWS IAM key with minimal permissions causes limited damage. An OpenAI Organization key without spending caps can generate tens of thousands of dollars of charges in hours — charges that may not be immediately reversible. The cost of a secrets scanning gate is negligible compared to the potential remediation cost.
Benefits
Prevents credentials from entering git history where they become permanently difficult to remove.
Detects secrets in vcrpy cassette files and other test fixtures that developers may not realize contain API responses with Bearer tokens.
Provides an automated audit trail of detected secrets, linked to commits and contributors.
Pre-commit hooks catch secrets before they leave the developer's machine, providing the fastest possible feedback.
Curated detector libraries cover hundreds of known API key formats including all major LLM providers.
Entropy-based detection catches novel or custom token formats not covered by pattern libraries.
Trade-offs
False positives: high-entropy strings in legitimate test fixtures, sample data, or base64-encoded content can trigger entropy-based detectors.
Maintaining allowlists for false positives requires ongoing team effort to avoid scanner fatigue.
A secret that was committed and then removed is still in git history; the gate blocks future commits but cannot retroactively protect past exposure.
Pre-commit hooks can be bypassed with git commit --no-verify; they are a developer convenience, not a security guarantee.
Custom detector patterns for proprietary internal services must be maintained alongside the scanner configuration.
How it works
Pre-commit hooks

Pre-commit hooks run on the developer's local machine before each commit is created. The hook invokes a secrets scanner (typically detect-secrets or gitleaks) against the staged files. If any secrets are found, the commit is aborted and the developer sees a list of detected secrets with file paths and line numbers. Pre-commit hooks are installed via the pre-commit framework and enforced by an optional CI check that verifies the hooks are installed.
CI scanning on PR

The CI pipeline runs a full repository scan on every pull request using a more thorough scanner (truffleHog or gitleaks) that checks not only changed files but the entire git history of the branch. This catches secrets introduced in earlier commits that were subsequently removed — a common pattern where a developer accidentally commits a key, notices, removes it in a followup commit, but does not realize the key is still in the history. A CI scan of git history catches this case.
Detector libraries and custom patterns

Modern secrets scanners ship with curated detector libraries covering hundreds of known secret formats. truffleHog's detector library includes detectors for OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, HuggingFace, Pinecone, Weaviate, LangSmith, and dozens of other AI platform services. Teams must also maintain custom detector patterns for any proprietary internal services or API key formats not covered by the public detector library.
Allowlist management

When a secrets scanner produces a false positive — a high-entropy string in a test fixture that is not actually a credential — the team must add it to an allowlist so the scanner stops flagging it. Allowlists are stored as a .secrets.baseline file (detect-secrets) or as inline suppression comments. Each allowlist entry must include a human-readable justification to prevent suppression creep.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Secrets scanning CI gate: pre-commit hook blocks at commit time, CI scanner blocks at PR merge, covering all major AI provider key formats

content.text: The diagram shows the two-layer defense. Pre-commit hooks catch secrets before they leave the developer's machine. The CI scanner catches anything that slipped through and blocks it at PR merge. The right panel shows AI-specific secret types that standard scanners must be configured to detect. The response protocol at the bottom covers the immediate steps required when a secret is detected in an existing commit.
When to use it
Use secrets scanning in every repository that contains or may contain LLM API credentials, vector store tokens, or evaluation platform keys — which is every AI repository.
Install pre-commit hooks for all developers working on AI projects, not as optional tooling.
Run CI secrets scanning on every PR in both private and public repositories.
Enable GitHub Advanced Security secret scanning for additional coverage on GitHub-hosted repositories (free for public repos, paid for private).
Run a periodic full-history scan of all repositories, not just changed files, to catch secrets introduced before scanning was enabled.
How to implement

# .pre-commit-config.yaml
# Pre-commit hooks for secrets scanning in AI repositories

repos:
  - repo: https://github.com/Yelp/detect-secrets
    rev: v1.5.0
    hooks:
      - id: detect-secrets
        args: ["--baseline", ".secrets.baseline"]
        # Exclude test cassette files from high-entropy scanning
        # (scrub them separately using cassette-scrubber tool)
        exclude: "tests/.*/cassettes/.*\.yaml"

  - repo: https://github.com/gitleaks/gitleaks
    rev: v8.21.2
    hooks:
      - id: gitleaks
        # Custom config adds AI provider patterns
        args: ["--config", ".gitleaks.toml"]

  - repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks
    rev: v5.0.0
    hooks:
      - id: detect-private-key      # catches private keys and PEM files
      - id: check-added-large-files  # prevents large binary files (model weights)
        args: ["--maxkb", "500"]

# .gitleaks.toml
# Custom gitleaks configuration with AI provider-specific patterns

title = "LearnAI Secrets Scanner"

[extend]
# Extend the default gitleaks config with upstream rule library
useDefault = true

[[rules]]
id = "openai-api-key"
description = "OpenAI API Key"
regex = '''sk-[a-zA-Z0-9]{20,60}'''
tags = ["ai", "llm", "openai"]
severity = "CRITICAL"

[[rules]]
id = "anthropic-api-key"
description = "Anthropic API Key"
regex = '''sk-ant-[a-zA-Z0-9\-_]{40,100}'''
tags = ["ai", "llm", "anthropic"]
severity = "CRITICAL"

[[rules]]
id = "huggingface-token"
description = "HuggingFace Access Token"
regex = '''hf_[a-zA-Z0-9]{30,50}'''
tags = ["ai", "huggingface"]
severity = "HIGH"

[[rules]]
id = "pinecone-api-key"
description = "Pinecone API Key"
regex = '''[a-f0-9]{8}-[a-f0-9]{4}-[a-f0-9]{4}-[a-f0-9]{4}-[a-f0-9]{12}'''
# Only flag when near 'pinecone' context
keywords = ["pinecone"]
tags = ["ai", "vectordb", "pinecone"]
severity = "HIGH"

[[rules]]
id = "langsmith-api-key"
description = "LangSmith API Key"
regex = '''ls__[a-zA-Z0-9]{32,64}'''
tags = ["ai", "langsmith"]
severity = "HIGH"

[allowlist]
description = "Allowlisted paths and patterns"
paths = [
    ".gitleaks.toml",           # This file contains patterns, not secrets
    ".secrets.baseline",        # detect-secrets baseline
    "tests/fixtures/sample_responses.json",  # documented fake API responses
]
# Allowlist clearly fake/test values
regexes = [
    "sk-test-[a-zA-Z0-9]+",    # explicitly fake test keys
    "sk-ant-test[a-zA-Z0-9]+", # explicitly fake Anthropic test keys
]

# .github/workflows/secrets-scan.yml
name: Secrets Scan
on:
  push:
    branches: ["**"]
  pull_request:

jobs:
  secrets-scan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0   # Full git history for complete scan
      
      - name: Run truffleHog (full history scan)
        uses: trufflesecurity/trufflehog@main
        with:
          path: ./
          base: ${{ github.event.repository.default_branch }}
          head: HEAD
          extra_args: >
            --only-verified
            --fail
            --config .trufflehog.yaml
      
      - name: Run gitleaks (staged files + recent commits)
        uses: gitleaks/gitleaks-action@v2
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          GITLEAKS_LICENSE: ${{ secrets.GITLEAKS_LICENSE }}
        with:
          config-path: .gitleaks.toml
      
      - name: Verify pre-commit hooks are installed
        run: |
          pip install pre-commit
          pre-commit run --all-files detect-secrets || true
          # Verify baseline is up to date
          python3 -c "
          import json, sys
          with open('.secrets.baseline') as f:
              baseline = json.load(f)
          # Fail if baseline has unreviewed results
          unreviewed = [r for f in baseline.get('results', {}).values() for r in f if not r.get('is_verified')]
          if unreviewed:
              print(f'ERROR: {len(unreviewed)} unreviewed entries in .secrets.baseline')
              sys.exit(1)
          "
Examples

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# scripts/scrub-cassettes.py
# Scrub API credentials from vcrpy cassette files before committing

import re
import sys
from pathlib import Path

CASSETTE_DIR = Path("tests") / "integration" / "cassettes"

# Patterns to scrub from cassette files (replace with placeholder)
PATTERNS = [
    (re.compile(r"(Authorization:\s+Bearer\s+)(sk-[a-zA-Z0-9]{20,})"), r"\1REDACTED_API_KEY"),
    (re.compile(r"(Authorization:\s+Bearer\s+)(sk-ant-[a-zA-Z0-9\-_]{40,})"), r"\1REDACTED_ANTHROPIC_KEY"),
    (re.compile(r"(x-api-key:\s+)(sk-[a-zA-Z0-9]{20,})"), r"\1REDACTED_API_KEY"),
    (re.compile(r'"api_key":\s+"(sk-[a-zA-Z0-9]{20,})"'), '"api_key": "REDACTED_API_KEY"'),
]

def scrub_cassette(path: Path) -> int:
    content = path.read_text()
    original = content
    for pattern, replacement in PATTERNS:
        content = pattern.sub(replacement, content)
    if content != original:
        path.write_text(content)
        return 1
    return 0

def main():
    cassette_files = list(CASSETTE_DIR.rglob("*.yaml"))
    scrubbed = sum(scrub_cassette(f) for f in cassette_files)
    if scrubbed:
        print(f"Scrubbed {scrubbed} cassette file(s).")
        print("Review changes with: git diff tests/integration/cassettes/")
    else:
        print("No credentials found in cassettes.")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Real-world example

An AI startup's backend repository was accidentally set to public visibility for 12 minutes during a GitHub settings migration. During that window, bots detected the OpenAI API key (sk-...) committed in a .env.example file four months earlier. The key was in git history even though the file had been deleted in a subsequent commit. Within 20 minutes of re-privatizing the repository, the team received OpenAI abuse alerts — $3,200 of API calls had been generated by the harvested key.

After the incident, the team implemented a three-layer secrets defence: pre-commit hooks using detect-secrets that block commits containing high-entropy strings; a CI secrets scanning gate using truffleHog that scans the full git history on every PR and blocks merges; and a GitHub Advanced Security secret scanning configuration that monitors the repository continuously and sends real-time alerts on any detected credential format. They also set up hard spending caps on all API keys and implemented a key rotation policy that rotates every key every 90 days regardless of exposure status.
Common mistakes
Committing .env files containing real credentials for 'convenience' — even if .env is in .gitignore, a git add -f .env or an IDE auto-stage can commit it.
Storing real API keys in vcrpy cassette files — cassettes record HTTP headers including Authorization: Bearer <key>.
Not scanning git history, only staged files — secrets in earlier commits survive despite being removed.
Setting allowlists too broadly (e.g., excluding entire test directories) — creates blind spots for secrets in test fixtures.
Installing secrets scanning only in CI without pre-commit hooks — delays feedback to PR review time rather than commit time.
Using placeholder values that match secret patterns (sk-test-123456789012345678901234567) — they fail the scanner and train developers to bypass it.
Best practices
Use a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, GitHub Actions Secrets) for all credentials — never store secrets in any file in the repository.
Scrub vcrpy cassette files before committing them using an automated script that replaces Authorization headers with REDACTED placeholders.
Require pre-commit hooks to be installed for all contributors; add a CI check that runs detect-secrets --check to verify the baseline is current.
Set hard spending caps on all LLM API keys: a cap of $100-500 for development keys prevents accidental runaway spend even if credentials are leaked.
Enable GitHub Advanced Security secret scanning for all repositories (free for public repos) as a continuous monitoring layer beyond CI.
Document the response protocol for detected secrets prominently in the repository's security runbook: revoke first, investigate second.
Rotate all API keys on a 90-day schedule regardless of exposure status — reduces the window of damage from undetected historical leaks.
Performance & scalability

Secrets scanning is fast. Pre-commit hooks scanning staged files complete in under two seconds for a typical AI repository. CI scanning with gitleaks on changed files takes 5-30 seconds. The expensive operation is full git history scanning with truffleHog, which for a repository with 10,000 commits can take 2-10 minutes. This scan should run on PRs (scanning from the branch point, not the full history) and as a periodic full-history scan (weekly or monthly) rather than on every push.

Allowlist management becomes the primary maintenance burden at scale. A team of ten developers working on an active AI repository may accumulate 20-50 allowlist entries over six months. Review allowlists quarterly and remove entries for files or patterns that no longer exist in the codebase.
Tooling & ecosystem
truffleHog
: the most comprehensive secrets scanner; built-in detectors for 700+ secret types including all major LLM providers; supports git history scanning.
gitleaks
: fast, configurable secrets scanner with a TOML rule format; excellent GitHub Actions integration.
detect-secrets
: Yelp's secrets scanner; baseline model tracks known false positives; well-suited for pre-commit hooks.
GitHub Advanced Security
: built-in secret scanning for GitHub repositories; automatically notifies when known secret patterns are found; free for public repos.
GitGuardian
: commercial secrets scanning SaaS with real-time monitoring and remediation workflows.
pre-commit framework
: Python tool for managing and installing multi-hook pre-commit configurations; standard mechanism for distributing secrets scanning hooks to development teams.

Security & reliability

The secrets scanner configuration files (.gitleaks.toml, .secrets.baseline, .trufflehog.yaml) are themselves sensitive artifacts — they document which patterns the scanner checks for, which paths are excluded, and which findings are allowlisted. These files should be reviewed in PRs with the same scrutiny as security-sensitive code. A malicious contributor who can add an allowlist entry can bypass detection for a specific secret pattern.

When a secrets scanner detects a credential in a CI run, the CI log must not echo the credential value. Configure scanners to report the file path, line number, and detector name — not the credential value — in CI output. This prevents the CI log itself from becoming an exposure vector. Most modern scanners mask credential values by default, but verify this in the scanner's documentation.

The response protocol when a secret is detected must be fast and non-optional. Revoke the exposed credential immediately — before investigating how it was committed, before communicating with the team, before opening a Jira ticket. A credential that has been in a public repository for even a few minutes must be treated as compromised. Rotate sibling credentials (other keys in the same environment) on the assumption that the same developer may have committed them elsewhere without detection.
Key takeaways

AI repositories contain a broader set of high-value secrets than conventional codebases: LLM provider keys, vector store tokens, embedding service credentials, and evaluation platform keys all require detection.
Pre-commit hooks provide the fastest feedback (before the credential leaves the developer's machine); CI gates provide the safety net.
Full git history scanning with truffleHog catches secrets that were committed and then deleted — the most common real-world pattern.
vcrpy cassette files frequently contain API credentials in Authorization headers; scrub them with an automated script before committing.
Spending caps on all API keys are a financial safeguard complementary to secrets scanning; a leaked key with a $100 cap causes limited damage.
Allowlists must be reviewed quarterly and documented with justifications; scanner fatigue from too many false positives is the primary reason teams disable scanning.
When a secret is detected in git history: revoke immediately, rotate siblings, rewrite git history, notify repository cloners.
